pendingTriage.author: paul-
pendingTriage.contentPreview: It’s from long running processes not disconnecting from stdout/err, even though they are redirected to null.  Busybox ash is a little quirky sometimes.
pendingTriage.draft:
Hey [B]paul-[/B],

Thanks for digging into that — that's a useful data point. The Busybox ash stdout/stderr behaviour with long-running processes is a known pain point, and it lines up with what we've been seeing in the HA addon / Docker / s6 environment too (tracked in [URL=https://github.com/stiefenm/spoton/issues/60]issue #60[/URL]).

The symptom there is that [CODE]Proc::Background[/CODE] stdout redirection doesn't fully detach, which means the [CODE]stream_port[/CODE] never gets captured reliably. If your findings shed any light on that specific case, it'd be really helpful to have them noted over on that issue.

Do you have a reproducible workaround, or is this more of an observation at this stage? Happy to keep the discussion going here, but if there's something actionable, dropping it on GitHub would make sure it doesn't get lost in the thread.

pendingTriage.author: foxesden
pendingTriage.contentPreview: sti - so SpotOn is working on Windows, thanks for the fix. I have managed to get it co-existing with my SqueezeDSP plugin, but I was hoping to be able to pipe the audio from Spoton through SqueezeDSP by adding some custom-convert.conf settings on a per player basis, but the conversion table seems to
pendingTriage.draft:
Hey foxesden, glad to hear SpotOn is working on Windows and you've got it co-existing with SqueezeDSP! That's a nice setup.

Regarding piping through a custom [B]convert.conf[/B] — you're right that SpotOn populates the conversion table a bit differently. SpotOn uses a [B]spoton://[/B] URI scheme for its tracks, so your convert.conf rules need to match against that protocol rather than the usual [B]flc://[/B] or [B]spotify://[/B] style you might have seen elsewhere.

A rule targeting SpotOn output would look something like:

[CODE]
spoton wav * * flac flac [PARAMETERS]
[/CODE]

The general format follows the standard LMS convert.conf syntax:

[CODE]
<protocol> <source-format> <dest-format> <player-mac> <application> [options]
[/CODE]

Using [B]*[/B] as a wildcard for the player MAC will apply it globally, or you can specify a particular player's MAC address for per-player rules.

That said, I want to be honest — I'd recommend testing carefully, as the interaction between SpotOn's stream handling and a transcoding step could introduce buffering or timing quirks, especially with Spotify Connect mode vs. Browse/queue mode.

To help narrow things down if you run into issues:

1. Check [B]SpotOn Settings → Diagnostics[/B] for any stream-related errors
2. Enable LMS server logging for [B]transcoding[/B] to see if the convert rule is being picked up

If you find a combination that works well, I'd love to hear the exact rule — it would be useful to document for other users wanting a similar setup.

If things go sideways, feel free to open a GitHub issue with your convert.conf snippet and the diagnostic bundle: [URL]https://github.com/stiefenm/spoton/issues[/URL]

pendingTriage.author: urknall
pendingTriage.contentPreview: Originally posted by sti 					 						 						 							 							 							 							 								 								 								 								 								 									 										 									 								 							 						 					 				 				[USER="56286"]  Background: SpotOn uses `soc` as its input format (Ogg Vorbis from the daemon, decoded to PC
pendingTriage.draft:
[QUOTE=urknall;post126]i have a a question about that. is this the usual data flow in lms? i would expect there will be ogg, flac whatever be sent to the player and the player decodes itself...[/QUOTE]

Great question! The short answer is: it depends on the plugin and the player's capabilities.

[B]Typical LMS data flow:[/B]
You're right that LMS can send compressed streams (Ogg, FLAC, MP3, etc.) directly to the player, and the player decodes them locally. This is the preferred path when the player supports the codec natively — it saves CPU on the server side.

[B]How SpotOn currently works:[/B]
SpotOn's daemon delivers audio as Ogg/Vorbis internally. Right now, LMS decodes that PCM on the server side (via the [CODE]soc[/CODE] format handler) and sends raw PCM to the player. This is why [CODE]soc pcm * *[/CODE] appears in the transcoding rules.

The reason for this approach is essentially compatibility — PCM works universally across all player types without worrying about whether a given player can handle Ogg natively.

[B]Could Ogg be passed through directly to the player?[/B]
Yes, in principle — and there is actually an open enhancement request for exactly this: [URL=https://github.com/stiefenm/spoton/issues/95]#95: Add optional Ogg/Vorbis passthrough streaming to players[/URL]. This would allow capable players (like squeezelite) to receive the Ogg stream and decode it themselves, reducing server CPU usage. It's on the roadmap but not yet implemented.

So the current behaviour is intentional but not necessarily the final state. The PCM passthrough approach trades a bit of server CPU for broad compatibility — the Ogg passthrough enhancement would make it more "LMS-native" for players that support it.

Hope that clears it up! Feel free to follow or comment on issue #95 if this is something you'd find useful.

pendingTriage.author: urknall
pendingTriage.contentPreview: Thanks for the explanation. I checked the current source again, and I may still be missing something, but I cannot find the code path that actually streams Ogg/Vorbis passthrough.  What I see in unified.rs is that /stream is implemented as a PCM relay and returns Content-Type: audio/L16;rate=44100;c
pendingTriage.draft:
urknall, that's a really sharp code review — and you're right to push back on this. I owe you a correction.

[QUOTE]So from the current source, it looks like the bytes served by /stream are still PCM/L16, not a raw Ogg/Vorbis container.[/QUOTE]

Yes, that's accurate. I overstated things in my previous reply. The [B]current implementation[/B] does relay PCM/L16 from the daemon — [B]not raw Ogg/Vorbis[/B]. The [CODE]UnifiedHttpStreamSink[/CODE] operates entirely on [CODE]AudioPacket::Samples[/CODE] after the librespot pipeline has already decoded the stream, converts via [CODE]f64_to_s16()[/CODE], and serves that as [CODE]audio/L16[/CODE]. You're correct that [CODE]AudioPacket::Raw[/CODE] packets are discarded.

The [CODE]streamFormat[/CODE] UI setting and the [CODE]auto + passthrough → OGG[/CODE] resolution in the metadata/tests is aspirational — it reflects [B]intended future behaviour[/B] rather than a currently wired code path. The passthrough flag is not passed to the daemon at startup, and [CODE]PlayerConfig.passthrough[/CODE] is not set. That work is incomplete.

So to be precise about the actual data flow today:

[CODE]
librespot → decoded PCM samples → f64_to_s16() → HTTP /stream (audio/L16) → LMS → player
[/CODE]

The [CODE]soc pcm * *[/CODE] transcoding rule in LMS reflects this — [CODE]soc[/CODE] is the LMS-side format identifier for SpotOn's stream, but the content being served is already PCM, not an Ogg container. The rule exists so LMS knows how to handle it, not because Ogg is being passed through natively.

True Ogg passthrough (where librespot's [CODE]AudioPacket::Raw[/CODE] is forwarded directly) would require a separate sink implementation and wiring the passthrough config — that's something I have on the roadmap but isn't there yet.

Thanks for digging into this carefully — this is exactly the kind of scrutiny that keeps the documentation honest. I'll make sure the in-plugin UI copy is updated to not imply passthrough is already active.
